en.extract:
The Cape cormorant (Phalacrocorax capensis), also known as the Cape shag, is a member of the cormorant family. It is endemic to the southwestern coast of Africa, and classified as "Endangered" by the IUCN.  The Cape cormorant is most closely related to the great cormorant and Japanese cormorant.


Description
The Cape cormorant is a blue-black bird with a glossy shine. The entire body follows the same coloration. There is little to no sexual dimorphism, with both males and females measuring 61–64 cm and weighing 1.1-1.3 kg, along with a similar plumage. The beak is dark grey-black, and a deep yellow-orange gular pouch can be observed. The eyes are turquoise with a greenish eye-ring around them.
Both non-breeding and immature adults have duller plumage, with a brown throat for immature adults. Juveniles are browner than immature adults, with grey eyes and a black, white, or speckled gular pouch.
Males and females have a wingspan of around 260 mm. For breeding adults, the wings are bottle green.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Cape cormorant is one of the 12 species in the genus Phalacrocorax, one of the 7 genera in the family Phalacrocoracidae. Phalacrocorax diverged from its sister genus, Urile (composed of North-Pacific cormorants) around 10 million years ago.
Within the genus Phalacrocorax, three sub-groups can be defined. The Cape cormorant is believed to share one of the sub-groups with the great cormorant and Japanese cormorant. However, the great and Japanese cormorants are much closely related to each other than to the Cape cormorant.
The taxonomic position of the Cape cormorant is disputed in the scientific literature. Some authorities place the Cape cormorant and other related cormorant species in the genus Leucocarbo, a group of Southern Hemisphere cormorants loosely characterized by their blue eyes.


Habitat and distribution


Distribution
Cape cormorants have a non-breeding range extending from Lobito, Angola, to Maputo Bay, Mozambique. Their breeding range is smaller, reaching from Southern Angola to the Eastern Cape province of South Africa. Cape cormorants stay close to the Benguela Upwelling System, an area of high food availability.
There are around 57,000 breeding pairs of Cape cormorants in Namibia alone. However, overfishing in the Cape cormorant’s range has led to a shift in food sources. Anchovies, sardines, and rock lobster have all recently shifted south and east. Cape cormorants have a limited foraging range when breeding. As such, this resource shift has led to a strong decline in Cape cormorant numbers.


Habitat
Cape cormorants nest in colonies, in remote areas safe from predators such as the black-backed jackal and human disturbance. They benefit from man-made guano platforms off the coast of Namibia, established for economic purposes, as permanent roosting and nesting sites. Cape cormorants can also roost in large numbers on wide beaches, which reduces the risk of predation.
Other habitats for the Cape cormorant include coastal islands and rocks, lagoons, and coastal marine waters.


Behavior and ecology


Vocalization
Vocalization in Cape cormorants is mostly used during the breeding season. Its use is significantly reduced, or even absent, during the non-breeding period. There are five vocalization variations in adults, and three in chicks.
Adult Cape cormorants have different vocalizations for flying, courtship, maintenance, feeding, and the presence of a threat. Both sexes emit the same vocalization in all cases, except for courtship. During courtship, males emit a repeated, low-pitch cluck. Females respond with a “gra-gra-gra-…”. Once the pair forms a bond, the male stops clucking and uses a voice similar to the female’s.
Cape cormorant chicks have different vocalizations for hatching, asking for food, and the presence of a threat.


Diet
Cape cormorants commonly forage in flocks. Their most common prey are pelagic schooling fish, such as anchovies, pilchards, and mackerel. Cape cormorants are foot-propelled diving birds and can forage from 5m to 150m deep. They are one of the main pelagic fish predators in South Africa, along with the Cape gannet and the African penguin. However, their foraging ecology is relatively understudied in comparison to those two species.
The diving behavior of Cape cormorants is like most cormorants, with a foraging time of 5.5 hours per day, and dives lasting between 10 and 70 seconds. Cape cormorants stay close to their colonies while feeding, with most foraging occurring within 20 km.


Breeding
Cape cormorants usually breed in dense colonies in areas protected from terrestrial predators, such as islands, cliffs, and ledges. These colonies require a reliable source of pelagic fish to sustain breeding adults and their newborn chicks. The breeding season lasts from August to May, and peaks between October and February. Clutch size averages three eggs, ranging from one to five.
Mating is initiated through male courtship. Fights can occur between two males over nesting sites, which are used by males for courtship. Cape cormorants are monogamous, and both parents help in taking care of the eggs. A “pecking distance” is maintained between nests to avoid conflict with other cormorants.
Several factors influence reproduction rates. Cape cormorant breeding coincides with the seasonal availability of pelagic fish. When fish numbers decline, cormorant populations quickly follow and breed less by deferring their age at first breeding. An increase in fish populations causes the equivalent effect. Increased predation and outbreaks of avian cholera also play a role in Cape cormorant decline.


Threats


Predators
The Cape cormorant’s main predator is the black-backed jackal. Jackals only represent a threat in open sandpits and beaches, since they cannot reach artificial guano platforms and remote coastal areas. Most Cape cormorants that fall as prey to black-backed jackals are sick, injured, or weak, making this interaction a healthy regulator for the Cape cormorant population.
On the artificial guano platforms, Cape cormorants are hunted by great white pelicans and great cormorants. These species target unsupervised chicks when the parents are temporarily away. Other studies show that Cape cormorant chicks are also hunted by kelp gulls and Cape fur seals.


Human-induced
The IUCN now classifies the Cape cormorant as "Endangered" due to a very rapid decline in the population over the last three generations.  The number of breeding pairs has been shown to depend directly on the amount of food available, particularly anchovies and sardines. Food availability has varied a lot since the 2000s, potentially due to climate change warming the cold waters of the Benguela Upwelling System. Overfishing has also played its role in the decline of Cape cormorant populations. Sardine stocks recently collapsed in the northern part of the Cape cormorant’s range, leaving the seabird and other predators with fewer resources.


Conservation
The main conservation efforts underway for Cape cormorants are the protection of breeding colonies, mainly on specific islands under the jurisdiction of South African National Parks. However, one of the major challenges Cape cormorants face is food availability, with prey populations collapsing in areas of the Benguela Upwelling System. Certain studies recommend a comprehensive assessment of cormorant populations and how prey populations affect their numbers.
The Namibian Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources has already committed to considering all elements of the ecosystem when managing fish populations. This includes predators such as the Cape cormorant, and the species’ survival will be an important reason to create quota limitations on industrial fishing, among other measures.


Gallery


References


External links

 Data related to Phalacrocorax capensis at Wikispecies

Cape Cormorant page from the Animal Demography Unit at the University of Cape Town

Cape Cormorant page from the South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI)'s Red List of South African Species
Cape Cormorant page from Cornell Lab of Ornithology's eBird
Cape Cormorant page from BirdLife International's DataZone

The '''Cape cormorant (''Phalacrocorax capensis'''''), also known as the '''Cape shag''', is a member of the [[cormorant]] family. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the southwestern coast of [[Africa]], and classified as "Endangered" by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]].<ref name="IUCN" />  The Cape cormorant is most closely related to the [[great cormorant]] and [[Japanese cormorant]].<ref name=":0" />

== Description ==
The Cape cormorant is a blue-black [[bird]] with a glossy shine.<ref name=":6" /> The entire body follows the same coloration. There is little to no [[sexual dimorphism]], with both males and females measuring 61–64 cm and weighing 1.1-1.3 kg, along with a similar [[plumage]].<ref name=":6" /> The [[beak]] is dark grey-black, and a deep yellow-orange [[Gular skin|gular pouch]] can be observed. The eyes are turquoise with a greenish [[eye-ring]] around them.

Both non-breeding and immature adults have duller plumage, with a brown throat for immature adults.<ref name=":6" /> [[Juvenile (organism)|Juveniles]] are browner than immature adults, with grey eyes and a black, white, or speckled gular pouch.

Males and females have a [[wingspan]] of around 260 mm.<ref name=":2" /> For breeding adults, the [[wing]]s are bottle green.

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The Cape cormorant is one of the 12 [[species]] in the [[genus]] ''[[Phalacrocorax]]'', one of the 7 genera in the [[Family (biology)|family]] ''[[Cormorant|Phalacrocoracidae]]''. ''Phalacrocorax'' [[Divergent evolution|diverged]] from its sister genus, ''[[Urile]]'' (composed of North-Pacific cormorants) around 10 million years ago.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Kennedy |first1=Martyn |last2=Spencer |first2=Hamish G. |date=2014-10-01 |title=Classification of the cormorants of the world |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1055790314002334 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=79 |pages=249–257 |bibcode=2014MolPE..79..249K |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2014.06.020 |issn=1055-7903 |pmid=24994028}}</ref>

Within the genus ''Phalacrocorax'', three sub-groups can be defined.<ref name=":0" /> The Cape cormorant is believed to share one of the sub-groups with the [[great cormorant]] and [[Japanese cormorant]]. However, the great and Japanese cormorants are much closely related to each other than to the Cape cormorant.<ref name=":0" />

The taxonomic position of the Cape cormorant is disputed in the [[scientific literature]]. Some authorities place the Cape cormorant and other related cormorant species in the genus ''[[Blue-eyed shag|Leucocarbo]]'',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Johnsgard |first=Paul A. |url=http://archive.org/details/cormorantsdarter0000john |title=Cormorants, darters, and pelicans of the world |date=1993 |publisher=Smithsonian Institution Press |location=Washington, D.C. |others=Internet Archive |isbn=978-1-56098-216-6}}</ref> a group of [[Southern Hemisphere]] cormorants loosely characterized by their blue eyes.<ref name=":0" />

== Habitat and distribution ==

=== Distribution ===
Cape cormorants have a non-breeding range extending from [[Lobito|Lobito, Angola]], to [[Maputo Bay|Maputo Bay, Mozambique]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Crawford |first1=RJM |last2=Randall |first2=RM |last3=Cook |first3=TR |last4=Ryan |first4=PG |last5=Dyer |first5=BM |last6=Fox |first6=R |last7=Geldenhuys |first7=D |last8=Huisamen |first8=J |last9=McGeorge |first9=C |last10=Smith |first10=MK |last11=Upfold |first11=L |last12=Visagie |first12=J |last13=Waller |first13=LJ |last14=Whittington |first14=PA |last15=Wilke |first15=CG |date=2016-09-23 |title=Cape cormorants decrease, move east and adapt foraging strategies following eastward displacement of their main prey |url=https://doi.org/10.2989/1814232X.2016.1202861 |journal=African Journal of Marine Science |volume=38 |issue=3 |pages=373–383 |doi=10.2989/1814232X.2016.1202861 |bibcode=2016AfJMS..38..373C |issn=1814-232X}}</ref> Their breeding range is smaller, reaching from [[Angola|Southern Angola]] to the [[Eastern Cape]] province of [[South Africa]]. Cape cormorants stay close to the [[Benguela Current|Benguela Upwelling System]], an area of high food availability.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last1=Crawford |first1=Robert J. M. |last2=Makhado |first2=Azwianewi B. |last3=Whittington |first3=Philip A. |last4=Randall |first4=Rod M. |last5=Oosthuizen |first5=W. Herman |last6=Waller |first6=Lauren J. |date=2015-02-18 |title=A changing distribution of seabirds in South Africa—the possible impact of climate and its consequences |journal=Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution |language=English |volume=3 |page=10 |doi=10.3389/fevo.2015.00010 |bibcode=2015FrEEv...3...10C |doi-access=free |issn=2296-701X}}</ref>

There are around 57,000 [[breeding pair]]s of Cape cormorants in [[Namibia]] alone.<ref name=":3" /> However, overfishing in the Cape cormorant’s range has led to a shift in food sources.<ref name=":5" /> [[Anchovy|Anchovies]], [[sardine]]s, and [[Spiny lobster|rock lobster]] have all recently shifted south and east. Cape cormorants have a limited [[foraging]] range when breeding. As such, this resource shift has led to a strong decline in Cape cormorant numbers.<ref name=":4" />

=== Habitat ===
Cape cormorants nest in [[Colony (biology)|colonies]], in remote areas safe from [[Predation|predators]] such as the [[black-backed jackal]] and human disturbance.<ref name=":2" /> They benefit from [[Bird Island (Namibia)|man-made guano platforms]] off the coast of Namibia, established for economic purposes, as permanent roosting and nesting sites. Cape cormorants can also roost in large numbers on wide beaches, which reduces the risk of predation.<ref name=":3" />

Other habitats for the Cape cormorant include coastal islands and rocks, [[lagoon]]s, and coastal marine waters.<ref name=":3" />

== Behavior and ecology ==

=== Vocalization ===
Vocalization in Cape cormorants is mostly used during the [[Seasonal breeder|breeding season]]. Its use is significantly reduced, or even absent, during the non-breeding period. There are five vocalization variations in adults, and three in chicks.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Berry |first=H. H. |date=1976 |title=Physiological and behavioural ecology of the Cape cormorant Phalacrocorax capensis Part 1 |url=https://journals.co.za/doi/abs/10.10520/AJA10115498_107 |journal=Madoqua |volume=1976 |issue=4 |pages=5–30 |hdl=10520/AJA10115498_107}}</ref>

Adult Cape cormorants have different vocalizations for flying, [[Courtship display|courtship]], maintenance, feeding, and the presence of a threat.<ref name=":2" /> Both sexes emit the same vocalization in all cases, except for courtship. During courtship, males emit a repeated, low-pitch cluck. Females respond with a “gra-gra-gra-…”. Once the pair forms a bond, the male stops clucking and uses a voice similar to the female’s.<ref name=":2" />

Cape cormorant chicks have different vocalizations for hatching, asking for food, and the presence of a threat.<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Cape Cormorants (Phalacrocorax capensis) big flock going out for fish ... (46450393482).jpg|thumb|309x309px|Cape cormorants [[foraging]] in a flock in [[Western Cape]], South Africa]]

=== Diet ===
Cape cormorants commonly [[Foraging|forage]] in flocks. Their most common prey are [[Pelagic fish|pelagic]] [[Shoaling and schooling|schooling]] fish, such as [[Anchovy|anchovies]], [[Sardine|pilchards]], and [[mackerel]].<ref name=":6">{{Cite book |last=Cook |first=Timothée |title=The 2015 Eskom Red Data Book of Birds of South Africa, Lesotho and Swaziland |date=November 1, 2015 |publisher=Birdlife South Africa |isbn=978-0-620-68259-6 |pages=107–109}}</ref> Cape cormorants are [[Diving bird|foot-propelled diving birds]] and can forage from 5m to 150m deep.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Ryan |first1=P. G. |last2=Pichegru |first2=L. |last3=Ropert-Coudert |first3=Y. |last4=Grémillet |first4=D. |last5=Kato |first5=A. |date=2010 |title=On a wing and a prayer: the foraging ecology of breeding Cape cormorants |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1469-7998.2009.00637.x |journal=Journal of Zoology |language=en |volume=280 |issue=1 |pages=25–32 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.2009.00637.x |issn=1469-7998}}</ref> They are one of the main pelagic fish predators in South Africa, along with the [[Cape gannet]] and the [[African penguin]].<ref name=":1" /> However, their foraging ecology is relatively understudied in comparison to those two species.

The diving behavior of Cape cormorants is like most cormorants, with a foraging time of 5.5 hours per day, and dives lasting between 10 and 70 seconds.<ref name=":1" /> Cape cormorants stay close to their colonies while feeding, with most foraging occurring within 20 km.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hamann |first1=MH |last2=Grémillet |first2=D |last3=Ryan |first3=PG |last4=Bonadonna |first4=F |last5=van der Lingen |first5=CD |last6=Pichegru |first6=L |date=2012-08-01 |title=A hard-knock life: the foraging ecology of Cape cormorants amidst shifting prey resources and industrial fishing pressure |url=https://doi.org/10.2989/1814232X.2012.709960 |journal=African Journal of Marine Science |volume=34 |issue=2 |pages=233–240 |doi=10.2989/1814232X.2012.709960 |bibcode=2012AfJMS..34..233H |issn=1814-232X}}</ref>

=== Breeding ===
Cape cormorants usually breed in dense [[Colony (biology)|colonies]] in areas protected from terrestrial [[Predation|predators]], such as islands, cliffs, and ledges.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last1=Simmons |first1=Robert |title=Birds to watch in Namibia: red, rare and endemic species |last2=Brown |first2=Christopher J |last3=Kemper |first3=Jessica |date=May 2015 |publisher=Ministry of Environment and Tourism, Namibia Nature Foundation |isbn=978-9-9945-0082-6 |pages=158–160}}</ref> These colonies require a reliable source of [[pelagic fish]] to sustain breeding adults and their newborn chicks.<ref name=":1" /> The [[Seasonal breeder|breeding season]] lasts from August to May, and peaks between October and February. [[Clutch (eggs)|Clutch size]] averages three eggs, ranging from one to five.<ref name=":3" />

Mating is initiated through male [[Courtship display|courtship]]. Fights can occur between two males over nesting sites, which are used by males for courtship. Cape cormorants are [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]], and both parents help in taking care of the eggs. A “pecking distance” is maintained between nests to avoid conflict with other cormorants.<ref name=":2" />

Several factors influence reproduction rates. Cape cormorant breeding coincides with the seasonal availability of [[pelagic fish]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Crawford |first1=Robert J. M. |last2=Shelton |first2=Peter A. |date=1978-09-01 |title=Pelagic fish and seabird interrelationships off the coasts of South West and South Africa |url=https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/0006-3207%2878%2990026-5 |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=85–109 |doi=10.1016/0006-3207(78)90026-5 |bibcode=1978BCons..14...85C |issn=0006-3207}}</ref> When fish numbers decline, cormorant populations quickly follow and breed less by deferring their age at first breeding.<ref name=":4" /> An increase in fish populations causes the equivalent effect. Increased [[predation]] and outbreaks of [[Fowl cholera|avian cholera]] also play a role in Cape cormorant decline.<ref name=":1" />

== Threats ==

=== Predators ===
The Cape cormorant’s main [[Predation|predator]] is the [[black-backed jackal]].<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal |last=Berry |first=H. H. |date=1976 |title=Physiological and behavioural ecology of the Cape cormorant Phalacrocorax capensis Part 2 |url=https://journals.co.za/doi/10.10520/AJA10115498_108 |journal=Madoqua |volume=1976 |issue=4 |pages=31–55 |doi=10.10520/AJA10115498_108}}</ref> Jackals only represent a threat in open sandpits and beaches, since they cannot reach [[Bird Island (Namibia)|artificial guano platforms]] and remote coastal areas.<ref name=":7" /> Most Cape cormorants that fall as prey to black-backed jackals are sick, injured, or weak, making this interaction a healthy regulator for the Cape cormorant population.

On the artificial guano platforms, Cape cormorants are hunted by [[great white pelican]]s and [[great cormorant]]s.<ref name=":7" /> These species target unsupervised chicks when the parents are temporarily away. Other studies show that Cape cormorant chicks are also hunted by [[kelp gull]]s and [[Arctocephalus pusillus|Cape fur seals]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Voorbergen |first=Anne |last2=Boer |first2=Willem F. De |last3=Underhill |first3=Les G. |date=2012 |title=Natural and human-induced predation on Cape Cormorants at Dyer Island |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/bird-conservation-international/article/natural-and-humaninduced-predation-on-cape-cormorants-at-dyer-island/827458DAF2DB4A44D754A48B75113C66 |journal=Bird Conservation International |language=en |volume=22 |issue=1 |pages=82–93 |doi=10.1017/S0959270912000032 |issn=1474-0001}}</ref>

=== Human-induced ===
The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] now classifies the Cape cormorant as "Endangered" due to a very rapid decline in the population over the last three generations.<ref name="IUCN" />  The number of [[breeding pair]]s has been shown to depend directly on the amount of food available, particularly [[Anchovy|anchovies]] and [[sardine]]s.<ref name=":6" /> Food availability has varied a lot since the 2000s, potentially due to [[climate change]] warming the cold waters of the [[Benguela Current|Benguela Upwelling System]].<ref name=":6" /> [[Overfishing]] has also played its role in the decline of Cape cormorant populations. Sardine [[Fish stocks|stocks]] recently collapsed in the northern part of the Cape cormorant’s [[Species distribution|range]], leaving the [[seabird]] and other [[Predation|predators]] with fewer resources.<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal |last=Erasmus |first=Victoria Ndinelago |last2=Currie |first2=Bronwen |last3=Roux |first3=Jean-Paul |last4=Elwen |first4=Simon Harvey |last5=Kalola |first5=Moses Shidalwomunhu |last6=Tjizoo |first6=Beau |last7=Kathena |first7=Johannes Nduvudi |last8=Iitembu |first8=Johannes Angala |date=2021-12-01 |title=Predatory species left stranded following the collapse of the sardine Sardinops sagax (Pappe, 1854) stock off the northern Benguela upwelling system: A review |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0924796321001184 |journal=Journal of Marine Systems |volume=224 |article-number=103623 |doi=10.1016/j.jmarsys.2021.103623 |issn=0924-7963}}</ref>

== Conservation ==
The main [[Conservation biology|conservation efforts]] underway for Cape cormorants are the protection of breeding [[Colony (biology)|colonies]], mainly on specific islands under the jurisdiction of [[South African National Parks]].<ref name=":6" /> However, one of the major challenges Cape cormorants face is food availability, with prey populations collapsing in areas of the [[Benguela Current|Benguela Upwelling System]].<ref name=":8" /> Certain studies recommend a comprehensive assessment of cormorant populations and how prey populations affect their numbers.<ref name=":6" /><ref name=":3" />

The [[Namibia]]n Ministry of Fisheries and Marine Resources has already committed to considering all elements of the ecosystem when managing fish populations.<ref name=":3" /> This includes [[Predation|predators]] such as the Cape cormorant, and the species’ survival will be an important reason to create quota limitations on [[Fishing industry|industrial fishing]], among other measures.<ref name=":3" />
